text: Obesity remains a major challenge for us in the twenty-first century and the aim of this video is to demonstrate to patients who are interested or are considering to have vertical sleeve gastrectomy what the procedure entails.
This patient also happened to have what's known as a hiatal hernia and therefore both she underwent a sleeve gastrectomy and hiatal hernia repair using the DaVinci XI robotic platform.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to tuck the liver away from the stomach, positioning it above the stomach to provide clear access for the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon inspects the hiatus, the junction where the esophagus meets the stomach, revealing a small hiatal hernia in the patient.
These are usually repaired if found at the time of surgery, as they may make a patient more prone to having reflux symptoms afterwards if they're not repaired.
The surgeon cleans the fat from around the stomach area and tucks the stomach, pulling it back into the abdominal cavity using surgical instruments.
As the definition essentially of a hiatal hernia is that your stomach has migrated into your chest.
The surgeon carefully frees the loose tissue around the stomach, ensuring complete separation from surrounding structures.
On the right side of the surgical field, the surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to simultaneously coagulate and cut blood vessels, preventing bleeding during the procedure.
The video shows an opening through the diaphragm, the breathing muscle, into the chest cavity.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the stomach, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissues.
The esophagus is visible as it enters the abdominal cavity, emerging from the diaphragm opening.
The surgeon identifies the white structure above the vessel sealer as the vagus nerve, a large nerve supplying most of the gastrointestinal tract.
The surgeon uses their left hand to lift and protect the vagus nerve while cleaning up loose tissue and fat behind the esophagus. The sizable opening through the diaphragm into the chest is visible.

And having a hole this big, if it's left unrepaired, then it will allow the stomach to migrate into the chest. As the stomach migrates into the chest, your stomach loses the ability to keep the acid from refluxing back into your esophagus. And that's what sometimes people describe as having this metallic taste in the back of your throat.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to tie and cut the blood supply along the greater curvature of the stomach, progressing towards the hiatus.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to tie and ligate small blood vessels around the esophagus as it enters the abdominal cavity, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suture to close the defect after ensuring all fat tissue has been pulled back and reduced, visibly tightening the area as the defect becomes smaller.
A tube is passed through the esophagus to assess the size of the opening, ensuring it is not too tight to avoid swallowing difficulties.
The surgeon removes the suture and inserts a sizing tube through the mouth, down the esophagus, and into the stomach, positioning it near the pylorus to ensure consistent sizing for the gastrectomy.

The surgeon uses a stapler with three rows of sutures to simultaneously suture and cut the stomach tissue. The stapler progresses sequentially towards the hiatus, with the sizing tube visible on the left side of the screen, ensuring the appropriate removal of stomach tissue.

The surgeon completes the transection of the stomach, removing the portion on the right. The newly formed stomach is submerged underwater for an air leak test. The anesthesiologist injects air to check for airtightness, ensuring the suture line is intact.
The surgeon displays the old stomach, which appears grayish due to the removal of its blood supply on the right side, preparing it for removal.
